### Process Stages

*What this means:* These four stages tell you where to start: Stage A checks structural trust, Stage B scores rule quality, Stage C checks alignment with real PR failures, and Stage D checks for noise.
*Why this matters:* The first failing stage is usually the right place to start fixing, because later scores often depend on it.

#### Stage A
Stage A checks whether the rule system is structurally trustworthy before deeper scoring. It is currently blocked by Validation Date Coverage, so the later scores should be read as diagnostic rather than clean.

Structural health is below threshold. Address process hygiene before deep rule scoring.

| Check | Status | Detail |
|-------|--------|--------|
| Mirror Duplication Rate | ✅ pass | 0% accidental duplicates (0/25); expected mirrors=0 |
| Mirror Sync Health | ⚠️ warn | healthy=7, drifted=0, orphan projections=1, source-only=1 (informational: source-only family: CLAUDE.md) |
| Governance Surface | ✅ pass | 10 governance canonical files vs 15 generated canonical files |
| Validation Date Coverage | ❌ fail | 0% of governance files include Last validated |
| Oversized Canonical Rules | ✅ pass | 4 canonical files exceed their recommended size budget |
| Drift Backlog | ✅ pass | path=17, date=25 |

#### Stage B
Stage B is the content-quality score for the canonical rules that actually matter. It passed, but Stage A Gate means you should read the score with some caution.

Stage B content scoring ran in advisory mode because Stage A failed.

| Check | Status | Detail |
|-------|--------|--------|
| Scoring Surface | ✅ pass | 10 canonical file(s) (governance-first) |
| Stage A Gate | ⚠️ warn | Stage A has failing checks; interpret Stage B score as diagnostic until Stage A is remediated. |

#### Stage C
Stage C checks whether the rules cover the failure modes engineers actually see in PRs. It is failing on Freshness Coverage, which usually means the rules are present but not fresh or not grounded enough.

Gap coverage is below threshold; recurring failures are not sufficiently represented in canonical rule text.

| Check | Status | Detail |
|-------|--------|--------|
| PR-Recurring Theme Signal Match | ✅ pass | 100% weighted signal match across PR-derived themes |
| PR Comment-to-Rule Alignment | ✅ pass | 100% weighted lexical alignment between PR comments and canonical rules |
| High-Severity Theme Signal Match | ✅ pass | 100% of high-severity PR themes have rule signal match |
| Critical Baseline Coverage | ✅ pass | 4/4 critical categories covered |
| Freshness Coverage | ❌ fail | 0% of scoring files include Last validated |

#### Stage D
Stage D checks whether the rule set is getting noisy, redundant, or hard to apply. It is failing on Context Load Pressure and Low-Yield Rule Ratio, which means the repo likely has too much instruction load or mirror-sync drift.

Overkill/noise pressure is high; repair mirror drift/orphan pressure and streamline low-yield rules before adding more.

| Check | Status | Detail |
|-------|--------|--------|
| Redundancy Pressure | ✅ pass | 0% pressure from accidental duplication |
| Conflict Pressure | ✅ pass | 20% pressure (mirror drift/orphans + keyword conflicts=0) |
| Context Load Pressure | ❌ fail | 799 always-on lines across scoring surface |
| Low-Yield Rule Ratio | ❌ fail | 10/10 scoring files miss Why or Examples |

### Rule Surface Segmentation

*What this means:* This shows how many rule files exist versus how many actually count after de-duplication and governance/generated separation. In this repo, the meaningful scoring surface is 10 canonical files, while 15 generated files exist mainly for distribution or mirroring.
*Why this matters:* This matters because a repo can have many copies, generated files, or projections, while only a smaller set actually represents the true rule system.
*Helpful term:* The canonical scoring surface is the set of rule files Anvil treats as the source of truth for scoring after removing duplicates and mirrored copies.

| Surface | Count |
|---------|-------|
| Discovered (raw) | 25 |
| Canonical unique | 25 |
| Canonical governance | 10 |
| Canonical generated | 15 |
| Duplicate mirrors removed | 0 |
| Expected mirrors (cross-agent projections) | 0 |
| Accidental duplicate mirrors | 0 |
| Duplication rate | 0% |
| Accidental duplication rate | 0% |

### Mirror Sync Health

*What this means:* This checks whether the same rule is being copied across multiple agent formats cleanly. This repo currently has 0 drifted mirror groups and 1 orphan projections.
*Why this matters:* When mirrored rule files are out of sync or missing a source of truth, different agents can follow different instructions without anyone noticing.
*Helpful term:* 'Drifted' means multiple copies of the same rule disagree. 'Orphan projection' means a copied rule file exists, but Anvil cannot find the canonical source file that should own it.
*Likely fix:* Pick one source-of-truth file for each mirrored rule, regenerate or sync the copies from it, and stop treating standalone files as projections unless their source file actually exists.

*Current surface:* Named source-only family: `CLAUDE.md`.

| Status | Count |
|--------|-------|
| Healthy | 7 |
| Drifted | 0 |
| Orphan projections | 1 |
| Source-only | 1 |

Mirror sync alerts:
- `ai-rule:optional`: A projected/copied rule file exists without a source-of-truth rule behind it: ai-rules/.generated-ai-rules/ai-rules-generated-optional.md. Practical risk: this file can drift silently because there is no canonical file to compare or regenerate from. Fix: either create the source rule file and keep this as a projection, or stop treating this file as a projection and make it the source of truth.

### Rule Quality Breakdown

*What this means:* These are the ingredients of the rule quality score. The current strongest area is rule file presence, while the weakest is hygiene / validation dates.
*Why this matters:* This matters because it separates 'the repo scored low' from 'this specific property of the rules is what pulled the score down.'
*Helpful term:* The format/helpfulness lane scores the canonical rule surface for clarity signals like rationale, examples, and size. It is not a requirement that every repo adopt AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, or Anvil-style headings everywhere.

### Gap & Overkill Metrics

*What this means:* This section balances two failure modes: missing rules versus too much noisy guidance. Healthy scores here mean the rules cover real problems without becoming instruction clutter.
*Helpful term:* A low-yield rule is a rule file that exists, but is weakly useful because it lacks enough rationale or examples to guide behavior reliably.
*Likely fix:* Strengthen or remove low-yield rules first, then split large always-on files so the rule set stays useful without overloading context.

### Guardrail Breakdown

*What this means:* This measures the engineering safety net around the rules: CI, tests, ownership, security, and drift resilience. The strongest area is CI discipline, while the weakest is review / ownership.
*Why this matters:* A strong rule set is still fragile if the surrounding engineering guardrails do not enforce it or keep it fresh.

## Top 5 Improvements

*What this means:* This is the prioritized change list synthesized from the audit evidence using repo-local heuristics.
*Why this matters:* It should read like an ordered backlog, not generic best-practice advice.

*Synthesis mode: heuristic*

### 1. Treat drift findings as active maintenance backlog [medium] (confidence 75%, impact 4/5)
Stale path/date references erode rule trust and model compliance.

Evidence:
- Path issues: 17
- Date issues: 25

Fix: Resolve all high-severity drift issues first, then add CI drift checks to prevent regression.

### 2. Raise guardrail baseline before expanding rule volume [medium] (confidence 70%, impact 4/5)
Without guardrails, additional rules increase complexity more than reliability.

Evidence:
- Missing: Secret scanning (gitleaks/GitHub secret scanning)
- Missing: Dependency vulnerability scanning (Dependabot/Renovate)
- Missing: Agent permission boundary config
- Guardrail score: 16/35

Fix: Implement the first two missing guardrails, re-run audit, and only then add new behavioral rules.

### 3. Address recommendation: 10 canonical scoring rule file(s) missing a "Why" section [low] (confidence 55%, impact 2/5)
The audit flagged this as a current high-value issue.

Evidence:
- ⚠️ 10 canonical scoring rule file(s) missing a "Why" section — explain the failure mode each rule prevents.

Fix: Implement this change in one focused PR with a before/after audit comparison.

### 4. Address recommendation: 2 canonical scoring rule file(s) missing examples (DO/DON'T) [low] (confidence 55%, impact 2/5)
The audit flagged this as a current high-value issue.

Evidence:
- ⚠️ 2 canonical scoring rule file(s) missing examples (DO/DON'T). Examples dramatically increase rule effectiveness.

Fix: Implement this change in one focused PR with a before/after audit comparison.

### 5. Address recommendation: 2 canonical scoring rule file(s) exceed their recommended size budget [low] (confidence 55%, impact 2/5)
The audit flagged this as a current high-value issue.

Evidence:
- ⚠️ 2 canonical scoring rule file(s) exceed their recommended size budget. Consider splitting or tightening the scored surface by file type.

Fix: Implement this change in one focused PR with a before/after audit comparison.

### Rule Effectiveness Status

*What this means:* This estimates whether the current rule surface can show measurable effectiveness instead of only intent. Current status: Unmeasured.
*Why this matters:* A rule that cannot name its failure mode, baseline, signal, and review point is hard to defend once the repo changes.
*Helpful term:* Instrumented means the rule surface names a failure mode, records a baseline (or baseline missing), picks one signal, and sets a follow-up interval.
*Likely fix:* Start with `AGENTS.md` — it already has the most instrumentation pieces in place. Add the missing pieces: baseline. Once that file has all four, re-audit to confirm the loop closes.

Status: **Unmeasured**

| Check | Coverage |
|-------|----------|
| Minimum instrumentation loop | 0/10 |
| Failure mode named | 3/10 |
| Baseline mentioned | 0/10 |
| Primary signal mentioned | 1/10 |
| Review interval mentioned | 1/10 |

No canonical rule file currently shows the full effectiveness loop. Add a failure mode, baseline, primary signal, and follow-up interval before claiming rule effectiveness.

Evidence:
- 0/10 canonical rule file(s) show the minimum instrumentation loop
- 3/10 name a failure mode or clear why-section
- 0/10 mention a baseline
- 1/10 mention an effectiveness signal
- 1/10 mention a review interval or follow-up point

## Recommendations

*What this means:* This is the merged action backlog from all scoring systems.
*Why this matters:* Use it as the shortest path from findings to action.

- 🔴 Stage A failed; treat Stage B scoring as diagnostic until structural checks are remediated.
- ⚠️ 10 canonical scoring rule file(s) missing a "Why" section — explain the failure mode each rule prevents.
- ⚠️ 2 canonical scoring rule file(s) missing examples (DO/DON'T). Examples dramatically increase rule effectiveness.
- ⚠️ 2 canonical scoring rule file(s) exceed their recommended size budget. Consider splitting or tightening the scored surface by file type.
- 🔴 Most canonical scoring rule files missing 'Last validated' date. Stale rules teach the model wrong things — worse than no rules.
- 🔴 8 high-risk stale always-on rule file(s) detected. Prioritize validation or splitting for `CLAUDE.md`, `.devagent/core/AGENTS.md`, `.devagent/plugins/ralph/AGENTS.md`.
- ⚠️ Overkill/noise pressure is moderate. Trim low-yield rules and keep always-on guidance lean.
- If you rely on GitHub merge queue, trigger CI on merge_group too.
- To reach 5/5 type safety, either add type-aware ESLint rules or enforce a dedicated extra-strict tsconfig lane in CI (for example `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` + `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`).
- Set explicit coverage thresholds in test config.
- Add CODEOWNERS for critical paths.
- Add a pull request template with review checklist.
- Prioritize secret scanning and dependency scanning first.
- Resolve drift backlog (path=17, date=25).
- 🔴 Rewrite or retire low-yield scoring rules: 10/10 scoring files miss Why or Examples
- 🔴 Trim always-on rule load on the scoring surface: 799 always-on lines across scoring surface
- 🟠 Validate or split stale always-on rules: 8 high-risk stale always-on rule file(s) need explicit remediation tracking. Start with `CLAUDE.md`, `.devagent/core/AGENTS.md` (missing Last validated + missing Why/failure mode).
- 🟠 Add Last validated dates: 2 canonical scoring file(s) are missing validation dates.
- 🟠 Add Last validated dates to governance rules: 0% of governance files include Last validated
- 🟠 Add Last validated dates to scoring rules: 0% of scoring files include Last validated
- 🟠 Add Why sections to canonical rules: 2 canonical scoring file(s) are missing Why sections.
- 🟠 Remove or backfill orphan mirror projections: 1 projection group(s) have no source-of-truth rule file.
- 🟠 Repair drifted or orphaned mirror rule projections: healthy=7, drifted=0, orphan projections=1, source-only=1 (informational: source-only family: CLAUDE.md)
- 🟡 Close missing guardrails backlog: 7 guardrails are missing.

## Missing Guardrails

*What this means:* These are engineering safety controls outside the rule text that are currently absent.
*Why this matters:* Missing guardrails increase the chance that a good rule set still fails in practice.

- Secret scanning (gitleaks/GitHub secret scanning)
- Dependency vulnerability scanning (Dependabot/Renovate)
- Agent permission boundary config
- Tool misuse blocking hooks
- Validation dates on rule files
- Automated drift detection tooling
- CI drift detection step
